children.name: Asterias
children.description: A genus of starfish from northern waters, typically with five arms and a rough surface covered in small spines. Asterias rubens is one of the most abundant echinoderms of the North Atlantic and a keystone predator of mussel beds.

children.name: Asterias rubens
children.description: The most familiar starfish of European Atlantic waters, found from Norway to Senegal on rocky and sandy substrates. Feed primarily on bivalve molluscs, everting the stomach into the shell to digest the prey externally.

children.name: Asterias forbesi
children.description: Common along the Atlantic coast of North America from Maine to the Gulf of Mexico. Plays a critical role in intertidal ecology by regulating mussel populations.

children.name: Asterias amurensis
children.description: A large, destructive invasive starfish native to the Sea of Japan but introduced to Tasmanian and Alaskan waters via ballast water. Considered one of the most damaging marine invasive species.

children.name: Pisaster
children.description: A genus of large starfish from the Pacific coast of North America, known for their ecological role as keystone predators in intertidal communities.

children.name: Pisaster ochraceus
children.description: A keystone predator of the Pacific intertidal zone. By preying on mussels it prevents their overgrowth of the substrate, maintaining biodiversity — one of the most famous examples of keystone predation in ecology.

children.name: Pisaster giganteus
children.description: Found on rocky shores from British Columbia to Baja California. Distinguished by blue rings around the base of its spines.

children.name: Leptasterias
children.description: A genus of small six-rayed starfish found in cold northern waters. Species are often brooding, retaining developing young in the oral cavity.

children.name: Leptasterias muelleri
children.description: A small brooding starfish of the North Atlantic and Arctic, typically found on hard substrates in the subtidal zone.

children.name: Leptasterias polaris
children.description: A large brooding starfish of Arctic and subarctic waters, reaching sizes unusually large for the genus.

children.name: Henricia
children.description: A genus of brightly coloured starfish with a smooth, fine-textured surface and typically five long slender arms. Feed on sponges and bryozoans by filtering suspended particles.

children.name: Henricia oculata
children.description: Found in the eastern Atlantic from Norway to the Mediterranean. Notable for its vivid orange or red colouration and preference for rocky substrates.

children.name: Henricia sanguinolenta
children.description: A circumboreal species found in the North Atlantic and North Pacific, typically on hard substrates in cold waters.

children.name: Coscinasterias
children.description: A genus of large, multi-armed starfish found in temperate waters worldwide. Known for asexual reproduction through fission.

children.name: Coscinasterias tenuispina
children.description: A Mediterranean and eastern Atlantic starfish with up to eleven arms, distinguished by blue spots on a grey or brown body. Reproduces by splitting into two individuals.

children.name: Coscinasterias muricata
children.description: Common in southern Australian and New Zealand waters on shallow reefs. One of the largest starfish in the region, with up to eleven arms.
